questions.question: Ravi found a very well-written essay on the Internet about global warming. He copied the entire essay and submitted it as his own school project without mentioning the source. What unethical practice has Ravi committed? What should he have done instead?
questions.answer:
Ravi has committed plagiarism — the act of using or imitating others' ideas and works without proper rights or without acknowledging the source of information. Plagiarism is a serious offence that results in severe penalties.

What Ravi should have done:
1. Obtained written permission from the copyright owner before using the essay.
2. Mentioned the source of information (citation) — acknowledging where he got the essay from.
3. Written the essay in his own words, using the original only as a reference.
4. If quoting directly, used quotation marks and credited the original author.

questions.question: Priya received an email saying: "Dear customer, your bank account will be blocked in 24 hours. Click here to verify your details immediately." The email had the logo of her father's bank. What kind of cyber threat is this? What should Priya do?
questions.answer:
This is a phishing attempt — a type of Internet fraud that aims to trick people into revealing sensitive information like credit card numbers and passwords. Phishing emails appear to come from a website similar to the original one but with duplicate image and name of the real company.

Priya should:
1. NOT click on the link.
2. NOT provide any bank details or personal information.
3. Remember that banks will never ask to send password or personal information by e-mail.
4. Delete the email immediately.
5. Never go to websites of banks by clicking on links included in an e-mail.
6. Tell her parents about the email.
7. Check that legitimate bank websites start with https:// and show a closed lock icon.

questions.question: Aman's classmate keeps posting mean and embarrassing comments about him on social media. Other students are also joining in. Aman feels very upset and does not want to go to school. What is happening to Aman? What steps should he take?
questions.answer:
Aman is a victim of cyberbullying — an act of threatening, harassing, humiliating, embarrassing, or otherwise targeting a person by another person using the Internet, digital technologies, or mobile phones. Cyberbullying can be very stressful to the victim, especially children.

Steps Aman should take:
1. Stop — Take five minutes to calm down, do not get irritated or terrified.
2. Block — Block the communication with the cyberbullies on social media.
3. Tell — Discuss the problem with his parents or a trusted teacher.
4. Don't respond to the bullies — responding may make things worse.
5. Keep evidence (screenshots) of the bullying comments.
6. Report the accounts to the social media platform.
7. Don't be an accomplice — friends who see cyberbullying should take a stand against it.

questions.question: Deepak's friend gave him a pen drive containing the latest version of an expensive video editing software. Deepak's friend said, "I bought one copy and made duplicates for all our friends." Should Deepak install this software? Why or why not?
questions.answer:
No, Deepak should NOT install this software. This is an act of software piracy — illegal copying of a software and its distribution without buying the license or copyright for the same.

When you purchase original software, you become a licensed user. You have the right to use the software on a single or multiple computers as per your license. You cannot install copies of this software on extra computers or give duplicate copies to your friends.

What Deepak should do:
1. Refuse to use the pirated software.
2. Buy his own licensed copy of the software if he needs it.
3. Explain to his friend that software piracy is illegal.
4. Look for free or open-source alternatives if the software is too expensive.

questions.question: Meera created a social media account and set her password as "meera2013" (her name and birth year). Within a week, someone hacked into her account and posted inappropriate messages. What went wrong? How can Meera create a better password?
questions.answer:
Meera's password was weak because she used her own name and date of birth, which are easy to guess. A hacker (cracker) gained unauthorized access to her account.

Guidelines for creating a strong password:
1. The length of the password should be at least 8 to 10 characters.
2. It should consist of alphabets in lowercase and uppercase, digits, and special characters.
3. Do not use your name, family member's name, or date of birth as these are easy to guess.
4. Avoid consecutive letters of alphabets and numbers.
5. Change your password at least once every month.
6. When you change your password, create a new (unused) one every time.
7. Avoid writing down your password or sharing it with anyone.

Example of a strong password: M33r@Str0ng! — contains uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters.

questions.question: Sanjay gets a phone call from someone claiming to be from his father's bank. The caller asks Sanjay to share the OTP that was just sent to his father's phone to "verify the account." What type of cyber fraud is this? What should Sanjay do?
questions.answer:
This is vishing — a fraudulent act of using phone calls or voice messages pretending to be coming from a genuine company but to steal confidential information. It is similar to phishing but done over the phone.

Sanjay should:
1. NOT share the OTP or any personal/financial information.
2. Hang up the call immediately.
3. Tell his parents about the call.
4. Remember that banks will never call and ask for OTPs, passwords, or personal information.
5. Report the phone number to the bank if possible.
6. Never trust callers who create urgency or pressure you to act quickly.

questions.question: Neha uses the same password "ilovecricket" for her email, social media, and online gaming accounts. She also checks the "Remember Password" option on the school computer. What are the risks? What should she do differently?
questions.answer:
Neha is making several security mistakes:

1. Using the same password for all accounts — if one account is hacked, all accounts are compromised.
2. Using a weak password without uppercase letters, numbers, or special characters.
3. Checking "Remember Password" on a public/school computer — anyone using that computer can access her accounts.

What Neha should do:
1. Use different strong passwords for each account.
2. Create passwords with alphabets in lowercase and uppercase, digits, and special characters.
3. Don't check the "Remember Password" option or the "Stay Signed in" option when working on public computers.
4. Make sure to logout properly when using a public computer.
5. Avoid typing your password while someone is looking over your shoulder.

questions.question: Karan's friend dared him to try logging into their teacher's email account by guessing the password. Karan tried several passwords but could not get in. His friend said, "It's okay, you didn't actually get in, so it's not a crime." Is Karan's friend correct?
questions.answer:
No, Karan's friend is NOT correct. Even attempting to gain unauthorized access to someone's account is considered hacking/cracking, which is a cybercrime.

Hacking refers to an act of gaining unauthorized access to a computer and/or network resources. A cracker (black hat hacker) is a person who gains unauthorized access to a computer system for the purpose of stealing or corrupting data.

Even though Karan did not succeed:
1. The attempt itself is unethical and may be illegal under cyber laws.
2. Computer hacking is punishable under IT Acts enacted in most countries.
3. Hackers should have written permission from computer or network owners to hack into the system (this is called ethical/white hat hacking).
4. Karan should respect others' privacy and never try to access someone else's account.

questions.question: Ananya loves downloading free games from random websites. After installing one such game, her computer became very slow and started showing pop-up advertisements everywhere. What has likely happened to her computer?
questions.answer:
Ananya's computer has likely been infected with adware and possibly other malware.

1. Adware is a program that places advertisements in your browser and allows pop-ups. This slows down the computer, giving the hacker enough time to get important information.
2. Malware is a malicious program that attacks a computer and harms the users' data. It spreads through the Internet because of files and e-mails being shared.

What Ananya should do:
1. Install an antivirus software (like Norton, MacAfee, Kaspersky, or Quick Heal) to detect and remove the malware.
2. Install an anti-adware program and update it regularly.
3. Never download software or games from unknown or unreliable websites.
4. Before downloading and installing any software, take permission of parents.
5. Keep her operating system and web browser updated to the latest version.

questions.question: During a group project, Arjun found a beautiful diagram in a library book. He took a photo, included it in his presentation, and wrote "Made by Arjun" below it. Is this correct? What should he have done?
questions.answer:
No, this is NOT correct. Arjun has committed plagiarism by claiming someone else's work as his own. The diagram is protected by copyright laws.

Copyright laws typically protect creative works such as books, movies, music, paintings, photographs, and software. They give the creator/owner exclusive right to control the reproduction or adaptation of such works.

What Arjun should have done:
1. Mentioned the source of the diagram (book name, author, page number) — this is called citation.
2. Written "Source: [Book Name] by [Author]" below the diagram instead of "Made by Arjun."
3. Obtained permission from the copyright owner if required.
4. Citation — mentioning an acknowledgment of the source — is the simplest technique to avoid plagiarism.

questions.question: Tina filled out an online form on an unknown website to win a "free iPhone." The form asked for her name, school name, home address, phone number, and her parent's email. What risks has Tina exposed herself to?
questions.answer:
Tina has put herself at serious risk by sharing personal information on an unknown website. The risks include:

1. Leakage of private information — the Internet can be misused by dishonest users to steal private and sensitive information.
2. Phishing — the website may be a phishing scam designed to collect personal data.
3. Identity theft — criminals could use her information to steal her identity.
4. Spam — her parent's email may now receive unwanted and unsolicited emails.
5. Cyberstalking — sharing address and phone number could lead to stalking.

What Tina should have done:
1. Not shared personal information on unknown websites.
2. Do not share your personal information without your parent's permission.
3. Consulted her parents before filling any online forms.
4. Remembered that "free" offers online are often scams.
5. Entered sensitive data only on secured websites that start with https:// and show a closed lock icon.

questions.question: Rahul noticed that whenever he searches for something online, he starts seeing advertisements related to that search on every website he visits. How is this happening?
questions.answer:
This is happening because of cookies. A cookie is a small piece of data or a program that collects the information of your online activity and reports back to the host. This information may be used for marketing purposes.

Websites track Rahul's browsing behavior through cookies and use this data to show targeted advertisements.

How to protect online privacy:
1. You can protect your online privacy by disabling the cookies in the web browser being used.
2. For sensitive information, always use the 'private' or 'incognito' mode of the web browser.
3. Delete the history of your web browser periodically.
4. Clear the cache of your web browser regularly.
5. You can set the privacy level in social media websites to control what information is shared.

questions.question: Kavita's 10-year-old brother wants to create an account on a social networking site. The site says users must be at least 13 years old. Kavita's brother wants to fake his age. Should Kavita allow him to do this?
questions.answer:
No, Kavita should NOT allow her brother to fake his age. Many social networking sites prescribe a minimum age limit for membership. Do not acquire the membership by faking your age.

This age limit exists because of the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) — a law created to protect the privacy of children under 13 years of age. According to this Act, a website operator should seek verification of information of children from their parent or guardian.

What Kavita should do:
1. Explain to her brother why the age limit exists — to protect children.
2. Tell her parents about her brother's request.
3. Wait until he is old enough to create an account.
4. Talk to parents and teachers about safe and appropriate websites for his age.
5. Never lie about personal information online — always maintain your online identity truthfully.

questions.question: Vikram's inbox is flooded with hundreds of emails offering miracle medicines, lottery prizes, and discount coupons. He did not sign up for any of these. Some emails have attachments. What should Vikram do?
questions.answer:
Vikram is receiving spam — unsolicited and unwanted e-mail. Spam may carry advertisements or serve as a means of spreading malware such as viruses and worms.

The problems caused by spamming include:
1. Wastage of user's time in reading unwanted e-mails.
2. Wastage of network bandwidth.
3. Danger of getting malware infection (especially from attachments).

What Vikram should do:
1. Delete spam mails without reading them.
2. NEVER open attachments from unknown senders — they may contain malware.
3. Use the spam filter of his e-mail program to block unwanted e-mails.
4. Do not give his e-mail address to unknown people in the future.
5. Do not enter his e-mail address at unreliable websites.
6. Never open an e-mail from an unknown person, just delete it.

questions.question: Aisha wants to post a group photo from her school trip on social media. Some of her classmates in the photo do not want their pictures posted online. Should Aisha still post it? What ethical practice is involved here?
questions.answer:
No, Aisha should NOT post the photo without the consent of all classmates in the picture. This involves the right to privacy and computer ethics.

Every person has the right to privacy — the right to determine what sort of information about him/her is collected and how that information is used. A person also has the right to review his/her own personal information and modify it.

Why Aisha should not post it:
1. Don't post your videos or photos online without your parent's permission.
2. Do not share others' photos without their consent.
3. Careless sharing of information — kids can unknowingly share too much personal information that could be stolen by miscreants.
4. Be respectful and courteous with others — a basic computer ethics rule.
5. She should ask all classmates for permission before posting.

questions.question: Amit found that someone has created a fake social media profile using his photos and name. The fake profile is sending friend requests to people Amit knows. What type of cybercrime is this? What should Amit do?
questions.answer:
This is identity theft — a cybercrime where someone steals your personal information and pretends to be you. It may also involve cyberstalking — using the Internet to stalk or harass a person by labeling false accusations.

Cybercrime is any illegal activity conducted through the use of a computer and the Internet. It includes identity theft, forgery, defamation, mischief, etc. A cybercrime is punishable under IT Acts.

What Amit should do:
1. Tell his parents or a trusted adult immediately.
2. Report the fake profile to the social media platform.
3. Warn his friends not to accept requests from the fake profile.
4. Take screenshots as evidence.
5. If the fake profile is causing harm, report it to the cyber police.
6. Review his own privacy settings to prevent photos from being stolen.
7. Digital footprint matters — always maintain your online identity and protect it.

questions.question: A website offers free downloading of the latest Bollywood movies and songs. Lakshmi's friend tells her it is safe because "everyone does it." Is it safe and legal? What should Lakshmi know?
questions.answer:
No, it is neither safe nor legal.

1. Downloading pirated movies and songs is software/content piracy — illegal copying and distribution without buying the license or copyright. This is punishable under cyber law.
2. Such websites often contain malware — malicious programs that attack a computer and harm the users' data.
3. The website may install spyware — a program that surveys your Internet behavior and copies your passwords to use them for illegitimate purposes.
4. "Everyone does it" does not make it legal or ethical.

What Lakshmi should know:
1. Copyright laws protect creative works such as movies and music.
2. Using pirated content is illegal and unethical.
3. Free download websites are often unsafe and may infect your computer.
4. Use legal streaming services or buy licensed copies.
5. Install an antivirus software and update it regularly.

questions.question: During an online class, Rohan shared his screen and everyone could see his open tabs, including personal emails and social media. His teacher noticed and spoke to him about it. Why was the teacher concerned?
questions.answer:
The teacher was concerned about Rohan's digital footprint and online safety.

Digital footprint is the information about a particular user that exists on the Internet as a result of their online activity. Whatever you do online persists and establishes your online identity.

Concerns:
1. Rohan accidentally exposed personal information (emails, social media) to the entire class.
2. This is careless sharing of information — sharing too much personal information could be used by miscreants.
3. Personal email content and social media could contain sensitive data.
4. Others could misuse the information they saw.

What Rohan should do:
1. Close all personal tabs before sharing screen in class.
2. Keep personal information private.
3. Be careful about what information is visible to others.
4. Use the 'private' or 'incognito' mode for personal browsing.
5. Delete the history of your web browser periodically.

questions.question: Suresh received a message from his friend's social media account asking: "Hey, I'm stuck abroad and lost my wallet. Can you send me Rs 5000 through UPI urgently?" Should Suresh send the money?
questions.answer:
No, Suresh should NOT send the money immediately. This is likely a phishing or hacking scam.

What may have happened:
1. His friend's social media account may have been hacked — someone gained unauthorized access and is pretending to be his friend.
2. This is a common phishing technique where hackers send urgent requests for money from hacked accounts.

What Suresh should do:
1. NOT send any money through UPI or any method.
2. Contact his friend directly through a phone call to verify the message.
3. If the friend's account has been hacked, help his friend report the hacking to the social media platform.
4. Remember that messages creating urgency are often fraud.
5. Never share financial information based on online messages alone.
6. Tell his parents about the suspicious message.

questions.question: Divya's computer has become very slow. A pop-up keeps appearing saying "Your computer is infected! Click here to download FREE antivirus." Should Divya click on it? What should she do instead?
questions.answer:
No, Divya should absolutely NOT click on the pop-up. This is itself a type of malware/adware trick.

1. Adware is a program that places advertisements in your browser and allows pop-ups. This slows down the computer, giving the hacker enough time to get important information.
2. Clicking such pop-ups may install more malware, spyware, or viruses on the computer.
3. It could also be a clickjacking attempt — a phishing technique in which a user is tricked into clicking on an item that appears to be something useful but acts maliciously.

What Divya should do instead:
1. Close the pop-up without clicking on it.
2. Tell her parents about the issue.
3. Install a genuine, licensed antivirus program like Norton, MacAfee, Kaspersky, or Quick Heal.
4. Install an anti-adware program and update it regularly.
5. Keep her operating system and web browser updated to the latest version.
6. Install a firewall — a network security program to keep a check on incoming and outgoing information.
